title: Type 3c (Pancreatogenic) Diabetes with ABPA and Chronic Pancreatitis: Four Problems, One Patient
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: basal-bolus in pancreatogenic diabetes (why sulfonylureas/SGLT2i are hazardous and steroids will spike glucose), reading a total IgE >2500 in ABPA-S, the steroid + azole regimen and what 'remission' really means, fecal elastase and PERT, and the truth about cancer surveillance in chronic pancreatitis
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

relatedChapters.title: Complex Diabetes Management in Advanced CKD

patient.chiefComplaint: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 10%) with a rising total IgE; multi-system follow-up.
pmh: Chronic pancreatitis with pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes since 2018
Common bile duct stricture with CBD stent in situ

caseDescription: A 42-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis and pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes since 2018, a CBD stricture with an indwelling stent, bronchial asthma and serologic ABPA (ABPA-S) presents with HbA1c 10% on glargine 14 units, gliclazide MR 100 mg, voglibose 0.4 mg and dapagliflozin 10 mg, and a total IgE above 2500 IU/mL with documented dust-mite allergy. The resident wants to switch him to a basal-bolus insulin regimen and asks: what to do about the rising IgE, the best ABPA regimen and how to define improvement/remission/cure on long-term follow-up, whether fecal elastase is needed, and how to approach cancer prevention in chronic pancreatitis.
keyFindings: HbA1c 10% on a complex oral + low-dose basal regimen — basal-bolus insulin is appropriate, but the type 3c context changes which drugs are safe
Type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes: insulin-deficient AND glucagon-deficient → brittle, hypoglycaemia-prone; insulin is the mainstay
Total IgE >2500 with ABPA-S and dust allergy — total IgE is the activity marker, but it must be read against the patient's own baseline, not a fixed cut-off
Treating ABPA with systemic steroids will sharply worsen glycaemia — anticipate and pre-emptively up-titrate insulin
Itraconazole interacts: it is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor (raises steroid exposure), needs LFT monitoring, and has erratic absorption
CBD stricture + stent + chronic pancreatitis raises the cholangiocarcinoma/pancreatic-cancer question — but routine cancer surveillance in ordinary chronic pancreatitis is not evidence-based
finalDiagnosis: Multi-system case: pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes that is poorly controlled and best managed with a basal-bolus insulin regimen (stopping the sulfonylurea, and reconsidering the SGLT2 inhibitor for euglycaemic-DKA risk), coexisting with active ABPA-S (total IgE >2500) requiring oral corticosteroid ± azole therapy — a combination that will transiently worsen glycaemia and demands coordinated insulin up-titration. Exocrine insufficiency should be objectively assessed (fecal elastase-1) and treated with enzyme replacement plus fat-soluble-vitamin care. Cancer concern centres on the CBD stricture (benign vs malignant) rather than a routine imaging-surveillance program, which is not indicated for ordinary chronic pancreatitis; smoking and alcohol cessation are the only proven risk-reducers, and new red-flag features drive targeted work-up.
learningObjectives: Construct a safe basal-bolus regimen for type 3c diabetes and justify stopping the sulfonylurea and reconsidering the SGLT2 inhibitor.
Anticipate steroid-induced hyperglycaemia when starting ABPA therapy and plan pre-emptive insulin adjustment.
Interpret a high total IgE in ABPA as a per-patient activity marker (track a new baseline and a ~25–35% fall), not a number to normalise.
Outline the oral-steroid ± itraconazole regimen for ABPA, its drug interactions and monitoring, and define exacerbation/response/remission (and why 'cure' is the wrong word).

Use fecal elastase-1 to diagnose exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and start enzyme replacement; understand why routine cancer surveillance in chronic pancreatitis is not standard and what actually warrants work-up.

items.title: Why pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes is not just 'type 2 with a pancreas problem'
items.content: Type 3c diabetes arises from loss of pancreatic islet mass (here from chronic pancreatitis), so it is characterised by deficiency of both insulin and glucagon, plus frequently pancreatic-polypeptide deficiency and concomitant exocrine insufficiency. The clinical consequence that matters most is brittleness: because the counter-regulatory glucagon response is blunted, these patients are unusually prone to severe and prolonged hypoglycaemia, especially once you intensify therapy. That single fact reshapes drug choice — agents that drive insulin secretion or independently cause hypoglycaemia/ketosis are hazardous, and insulin (carefully titrated) is the mainstay. Treating this man as if he had ordinary type 2 diabetes is the core trap.
keyPoints: Type 3c = islet loss → insulin AND glucagon deficiency (± PP deficiency, ± EPI)
Blunted glucagon ⇒ brittle, hypoglycaemia-prone — drives every drug decision
Insulin is the mainstay; don't manage it like ordinary type 2 diabetes

items.title: Why the sulfonylurea should be stopped in this patient
items.content: Gliclazide (a sulfonylurea) is a poor fit for type 3c diabetes for two reasons. First, it works by squeezing insulin out of beta cells that are already depleted in chronic pancreatitis — so its efficacy fades as islet mass falls, which is exactly why his HbA1c is 10% despite being on it. Second, and more dangerously, sulfonylureas cause hypoglycaemia, and in a glucagon-deficient patient that hypoglycaemia can be severe and protracted. Once you commit to a basal-bolus insulin regimen, continuing a sulfonylurea adds hypoglycaemia risk without meaningful benefit. Stop it.
keyPoints: Sulfonylurea relies on residual beta-cell secretion that is failing here
Adds severe/prolonged hypoglycaemia risk in a glucagon-deficient patient
Redundant once on basal-bolus — discontinue

items.title: Why the SGLT2 inhibitor needs a hard rethink (euglycaemic DKA)
items.content: Dapagliflozin in an insulin-deficient, often lean and malnourished type 3c patient carries a real risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis — DKA with only modestly raised or near-normal glucose, which is easily missed. The risk rises with the very things this patient may face: reduced carbohydrate/caloric intake, intercurrent illness, alcohol, and dehydration. Pair that with steroid therapy for ABPA and the metabolic stress climbs further. The cautious default in type 3c diabetes is to avoid SGLT2 inhibitors, and certainly to stop the drug around any acute illness, steroid course, fasting, or procedure (sick-day rules). If it is continued at all, it is with explicit ketone-monitoring counselling — but in this multi-morbid patient stopping it is the safer call.
keyPoints: SGLT2i risk: euglycaemic DKA — normal/near-normal glucose, easily missed
Risk amplified by low intake, illness, alcohol, dehydration, steroids
Default in type 3c: avoid/stop around illness, steroids, fasting, procedures

items.title: How to build the basal-bolus regimen and titrate it safely
items.content: Basal-bolus is appropriate at HbA1c 10%. Estimate total daily insulin conservatively in type 3c (≈0.4–0.5 units/kg/day given hypoglycaemia-proneness), split roughly half basal and half rapid-acting analogue divided across meals, and carbohydrate-count where the patient can. His current glargine 14 units is a low starting basal; expect to increase it. Titrate basal against the fasting glucose and each meal's bolus against the next pre-meal/2-hour post-prandial value, in small steps. Targets should be individualised and deliberately not too tight given the hypoglycaemia risk — a fasting target around 100–130 mg/dL and post-prandial under ~180 mg/dL is reasonable, relaxed further if hypoglycaemia appears. Structured self-monitoring (or CGM, which is especially valuable in brittle type 3c) and hypoglycaemia education are part of the prescription, not an afterthought.
keyPoints: Conservative TDI (≈0.4–0.5 U/kg/day) split ~50:50 basal:bolus analogue
Titrate basal to fasting, bolus to post-meal; small steps; individualised, not-too-tight targets
CGM + hypoglycaemia education are core given brittleness

items.title: Why starting ABPA steroids is the key glycaemic event to plan for
items.content: The most important interaction in this case is therapeutic, not pharmacokinetic: the oral corticosteroid needed for ABPA will substantially raise blood glucose, predominantly post-prandially and in the afternoon/evening with a morning prednisolone dose. In a patient already at HbA1c 10% this can be dramatic. The mistake is to start steroids and react to the hyperglycaemia afterwards; the correct move is to anticipate it — front-load the conversation about transiently higher insulin needs, intensify self-monitoring, pre-emptively raise bolus (and some basal) doses as the steroid starts, and then step the insulin back down in parallel with the steroid taper so he is not left over-insulinised and hypoglycaemic. This coordination between the endocrine and respiratory plans is the crux of managing this man well.
keyPoints: Steroids for ABPA cause marked, mostly post-prandial hyperglycaemia
Pre-emptively raise insulin (mainly bolus) when steroids start — don't just react
Taper insulin down in lock-step with the steroid taper to avoid hypoglycaemia

items.title: Why this is ABPA-S, and how the IgE >2500 fits the diagnostic picture
items.content: ABPA is diagnosed in a predisposed patient (asthma, or cystic fibrosis) by a cluster: a markedly elevated total IgE (commonly >1000 IU/mL), evidence of Aspergillus sensitisation (raised Aspergillus-specific IgE and/or a positive skin test), and supportive features such as eosinophilia, Aspergillus precipitins/IgG, and compatible radiology. The serologic subtype (ABPA-S) is defined by meeting these immunological criteria without central bronchiectasis on imaging — i.e. ABPA caught before fixed structural lung damage. His total IgE >2500 with dust-mite atopy is consistent with active ABPA; the job now is to confirm the Aspergillus-specific markers (specific IgE/IgG) and image the chest (HRCT) to confirm there is still no bronchiectasis and exclude mucoid impaction, because that staging drives both treatment intensity and prognosis.
keyPoints: ABPA = predisposition (asthma/CF) + very high total IgE + Aspergillus sensitisation + supportive features
ABPA-S = serologic criteria met WITHOUT central bronchiectasis (caught early)
Confirm Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG and HRCT to stage before/while treating

items.title: Why total IgE is tracked as a per-patient trend, not normalised to a cut-off
items.content: Total serum IgE is the single most useful marker of ABPA activity, but it is used as a relative, per-patient measure. After starting treatment you establish a new baseline (the value at 6–8 weeks) and follow the trend: a fall of roughly 25–35% indicates response, and a rise of similar magnitude from the patient's nadir flags an exacerbation. The classic error is to chase a 'normal' IgE — most treated ABPA patients never normalise their total IgE, and pushing therapy to force the number down over-treats them with steroids. So with his IgE >2500, the goal is a meaningful proportional fall and clinical/radiological improvement, not a specific target number.
keyPoints: Total IgE = best activity marker, used as a per-patient trend
Response ≈ 25–35% fall to a new baseline; similar rise from nadir = exacerbation
Don't chase a 'normal' IgE — most never normalise; doing so over-treats

items.title: How to treat active ABPA: oral steroids first, azole as the steroid-sparing partner
items.content: First-line treatment of an acute ABPA exacerbation is a course of oral corticosteroid (for example prednisolone starting around 0.5 mg/kg/day and tapered over roughly 3–4 months guided by symptoms, IgE trend and imaging). Itraconazole is the established steroid-sparing/antifungal partner: it reduces the fungal antigenic burden, can lower exacerbation rates and steroid exposure, and is typically given for around 16 weeks (often longer). It is added alongside or after the initial steroid response. Importantly the azole is not a substitute for steroids in a florid exacerbation — it complements them. Inhaled therapy for the underlying asthma continues throughout. Newer azoles (voriconazole/posaconazole) and anti-IgE/anti-IL5 biologics are reserved for relapsing, steroid-dependent or azole-failing disease.
keyPoints: Acute exacerbation: oral prednisolone (~0.5 mg/kg/day) tapered over ~3–4 months
Itraconazole ~16 weeks as steroid-sparing antifungal — complements, not replaces, steroids
Reserve newer azoles / biologics for relapsing or steroid-dependent disease

items.title: Why itraconazole needs respect: CYP3A4 inhibition, absorption and LFTs
items.content: Itraconazole is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor, so it raises exposure to many co-prescribed drugs — including corticosteroids (methylprednisolone and inhaled/oral budesonide especially), with reports of iatrogenic Cushing syndrome and adrenal suppression when an azole and a steroid are combined unwittingly. It also has erratic absorption: the capsule needs gastric acid and food, the oral solution is better absorbed on an empty stomach, and acid-suppressants blunt capsule uptake — checking a trough itraconazole level confirms therapeutic exposure. Monitor liver function (baseline and periodically) because of hepatotoxicity, review the full drug list for interactions, and be alert that combining the azole with the ABPA steroid can amplify both steroid side-effects and hyperglycaemia in this diabetic patient.
keyPoints: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor → raises steroid exposure (Cushing/adrenal suppression risk)
Erratic absorption — formulation/food/acid matter; check a trough level
Monitor LFTs; azole+steroid can worsen steroid effects and glycaemia

items.title: How to define exacerbation, response, remission — and why 'cure' is the wrong word
items.content: Use agreed, mostly objective definitions. Response: clinical improvement plus a ≥25% fall in total IgE at 6–8 weeks (with radiological clearing of any infiltrates). Exacerbation: clinical and/or radiological deterioration with a ≥50% rise in IgE from the patient's nadir. Remission: sustained clinical and radiological stability with no exacerbation for ≥6 months after stopping oral steroids. Glucocorticoid-dependent ABPA: disease that flares whenever steroids are withdrawn (or needs them for asthma control). 'Cure' is not a term used in ABPA — it is a chronic, relapsing immunological disease; the realistic and correct goal is durable remission with the least possible steroid exposure. Long-term follow-up tracks total IgE, symptoms and lung function, with HRCT for new or worsening structural disease.
keyPoints: Response = clinical improvement + ≥25% IgE fall at 6–8 weeks
Exacerbation = clinical/radiological worsening + ≥50% IgE rise from nadir
Remission = ≥6 months stable off steroids; 'cure' is not used — aim for durable remission

items.title: Why fecal elastase is needed here, and how to act on it
items.content: Yes — fecal elastase-1 is indicated. This man has chronic pancreatitis, pancreatogenic diabetes and a CBD stricture, so exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI) is likely and frequently under-treated. Fecal elastase-1 on a single stool sample is the practical screen: a value below 200 µg/g suggests insufficiency and below 100 µg/g indicates severe EPI (a watery/very loose sample can falsely lower it). EPI matters beyond steatorrhoea — it causes fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K) deficiency, B12 issues, osteoporosis and protein-calorie malnutrition, all of which also worsen the brittleness of his diabetes. Confirmed or strongly suspected EPI is treated with pancreatic enzyme replacement (lipase ~25,000–50,000 units with main meals, less with snacks, titrated to symptoms and nutrition), taken with food, alongside fat-soluble-vitamin assessment and bone-health screening.
keyPoints: Fecal elastase-1 <200 µg/g = EPI; <100 = severe (loose stool can falsely lower it)
EPI causes A/D/E/K and nutritional deficits that worsen diabetic brittleness
Treat with PERT (~25,000–50,000 U lipase/meal) + fat-soluble vitamins + bone screen

items.title: Why routine cancer 'surveillance' in ordinary chronic pancreatitis is not standard
items.content: Chronic pancreatitis does raise pancreatic-cancer risk, but the common reflex — enrolling every chronic-pancreatitis patient in 6-monthly CT/EUS/CA19-9 surveillance — is not evidence-based and risks harm from false positives and repeated radiation. Routine imaging surveillance is reserved for genuinely high-risk groups: hereditary pancreatitis (e.g. PRSS1) and familial pancreatic-cancer/known germline syndromes, where dedicated programs (MRI/MRCP ± EUS) are offered. For sporadic chronic pancreatitis the right strategy is risk-factor modification plus alertness to red flags, not a fixed surveillance calendar. CA19-9 is a poor screening test and is especially unreliable here because biliary obstruction/cholestasis (he has a CBD stricture and stent) elevates it non-specifically. Be careful not to over-promise a surveillance program that guidelines do not support.
keyPoints: Routine CT/EUS/CA19-9 surveillance in sporadic chronic pancreatitis is NOT standard
Dedicated surveillance is for hereditary/familial high-risk groups only
CA19-9 is unreliable for screening and falsely raised by biliary obstruction/stent

items.title: How to actually reduce and detect cancer risk in this man
items.content: Two threads. Prevention: the only proven risk-reducers are complete cessation of smoking and alcohol — both independently drive chronic-pancreatitis progression and pancreatic cancer, and this is the single highest-value intervention. Optimise nutrition and diabetes control; chemoprevention (aspirin, statins, metformin) has only weak, hypothesis-level signals and is not a guideline indication. Detection: the live oncological question is his CBD stricture — distinguishing a benign chronic-pancreatitis stricture from cholangiocarcinoma or a pancreatic head cancer. That is pursued with cross-sectional imaging (MRCP/CT), and where suspicion exists, ERCP with brush cytology/biopsy and EUS-FNA, not with a generic surveillance schedule. Finally, a relevant teaching point: new-onset or abruptly worsening diabetes can itself be the presenting sign of pancreatic cancer, so unexplained deterioration deserves imaging, not just more insulin.
keyPoints: Proven prevention = total smoking AND alcohol cessation; chemoprevention is not established
Focus detection on the CBD stricture (benign vs malignant) with MRCP/CT, ERCP brushings, EUS-FNA
Abrupt unexplained diabetes worsening can herald pancreatic cancer — image, don't just up-titrate

items.question: Why are sulfonylureas and SGLT2 inhibitors both problematic choices in type 3c diabetes?
items.answer: Because the physiology of type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes — loss of islet mass causing deficiency of both insulin and glucagon — makes each drug class hazardous in its own way. Sulfonylureas act by stimulating insulin secretion from beta cells that are already depleted, so efficacy is limited and waning; worse, the hypoglycaemia they cause is dangerous in a patient whose glucagon counter-regulation is blunted, so lows are severe and prolonged. SGLT2 inhibitors carry a heightened risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis in an insulin-deficient, often lean/malnourished patient — ketoacidosis with near-normal glucose that is easily missed, and the risk climbs with reduced intake, intercurrent illness, alcohol, dehydration and concurrent steroids. Insulin, carefully titrated with attention to hypoglycaemia, is the mainstay instead.
keyPoints: Sulfonylurea: relies on failing beta cells; severe/prolonged hypoglycaemia with glucagon deficiency
SGLT2i: euglycaemic DKA risk in insulin-deficient, lean, illness-/steroid-stressed patients
Insulin is the mainstay in type 3c

items.question: How should this patient's ABPA be monitored long-term, and when can you call it remission?
items.answer: Track three things: symptoms, total serum IgE as a per-patient trend, and imaging. Set a new IgE baseline at 6–8 weeks of treatment; a fall of about 25–35% with clinical and radiological improvement signals response, and you do not aim to normalise the IgE. Follow IgE roughly every 6–8 weeks while treating and then periodically; a rise of about 50% from the patient's nadir with clinical/radiological deterioration defines an exacerbation. Spirometry and HRCT (for new infiltrates, mucoid impaction or evolving bronchiectasis) round out monitoring. Remission is sustained clinical and radiological stability with no exacerbation for at least 6 months after stopping oral steroids. 'Cure' is not used — ABPA is chronic and relapsing — so the goal is durable remission on the least steroid possible, escalating to azole or biologic therapy if he proves steroid-dependent or relapses.
keyPoints: Monitor symptoms + total IgE trend (new baseline at 6–8 weeks) + imaging/spirometry
Response ≈ 25–35% IgE fall; exacerbation ≈ 50% rise from nadir
Remission = ≥6 months stable off steroids; no 'cure' — aim for durable remission

items.stem: A 42-year-old man with type 3c diabetes (chronic pancreatitis) has HbA1c 10% on glargine 14 U, gliclazide MR 100 mg, voglibose 0.4 mg and dapagliflozin 10 mg. As you move him to basal-bolus insulin, which medication change is most appropriate?
options: Stop gliclazide because of limited benefit and hypoglycaemia risk in glucagon-deficient type 3c diabetes
Increase gliclazide to 160 mg for tighter control
Add a second sulfonylurea for synergy
Continue all oral agents unchanged alongside basal-bolus insulin
items.explanation: In type 3c diabetes the beta cells are depleted, so the sulfonylurea adds little glucose-lowering but real hypoglycaemia risk, which is dangerous given blunted glucagon counter-regulation. It should be stopped when moving to basal-bolus insulin. Increasing or adding sulfonylureas worsens the hazard.

items.stem: Why is dapagliflozin a concern in this patient with pancreatogenic diabetes?
options: Risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis in an insulin-deficient, malnourished patient
It causes severe hyperkalaemia in chronic pancreatitis
It directly damages residual islet cells
It blocks itraconazole absorption
items.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors can precipitate euglycaemic DKA — ketoacidosis with near-normal glucose, easily missed — and insulin-deficient, lean type 3c patients facing reduced intake, illness, alcohol or steroids are at particular risk. The other options are not recognised effects.

items.stem: You plan to start prednisolone for this man's ABPA exacerbation. What is the most important concurrent action for his diabetes?
options: Anticipate steroid-induced hyperglycaemia and pre-emptively up-titrate insulin, then taper insulin as steroids taper
Stop all insulin while on steroids to avoid hypoglycaemia
Switch to metformin monotherapy during the steroid course
Make no changes; steroids do not affect glucose at this dose
items.explanation: Systemic steroids cause marked, mainly post-prandial hyperglycaemia. The right approach is to anticipate it — intensify monitoring and raise insulin (chiefly bolus) as the steroid starts — then step insulin back down in parallel with the taper to avoid later hypoglycaemia. Stopping insulin or relying on metformin would be unsafe.

items.stem: How should the total IgE of >2500 IU/mL be used in following this patient's ABPA?
options: As a per-patient activity marker — set a new baseline and track a ~25–35% fall as response, not a number to normalise
As a target to be driven into the normal range before stopping therapy
As an irrelevant value once Aspergillus-specific IgE is known
As a direct measure of his insulin requirement
items.explanation: Total IgE is the best activity marker in ABPA but is used relatively: establish a new baseline at 6–8 weeks, treat a ~25–35% fall (with clinical/radiological improvement) as response, and watch for a ~50% rise from nadir as exacerbation. Most patients never normalise IgE, so chasing a normal value over-treats with steroids.

items.stem: What is the first-line treatment of an acute ABPA exacerbation, and the role of itraconazole?
options: Oral corticosteroids are first-line; itraconazole is a steroid-sparing antifungal partner
Itraconazole monotherapy replaces steroids in all exacerbations
Inhaled corticosteroids alone are sufficient for an acute flare
Omalizumab is the routine first-line agent for every exacerbation
items.explanation: Oral corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone ~0.5 mg/kg/day tapered over ~3–4 months) are first-line for an acute ABPA exacerbation; itraconazole (~16 weeks) is the steroid-sparing antifungal that lowers fungal burden and steroid exposure but complements rather than replaces steroids. Biologics are reserved for relapsing/steroid-dependent disease.

items.stem: A fecal elastase-1 of 80 µg/g is reported. What does this indicate and what is the next step?
options: Severe exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — start pancreatic enzyme replacement and assess fat-soluble vitamins
Normal exocrine function — no action needed
Acute pancreatitis requiring hospital admission
Pancreatic cancer, mandating immediate surgery
items.explanation: Fecal elastase-1 below 100 µg/g indicates severe exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Management is enzyme replacement (lipase ~25,000–50,000 units with main meals) taken with food, plus assessment/replacement of fat-soluble vitamins and bone-health screening. It is neither normal nor diagnostic of cancer or acute pancreatitis.

items.stem: Regarding cancer in this man with chronic pancreatitis and a CBD stricture/stent, which statement is correct?
options: Routine 6-monthly CT/EUS/CA19-9 surveillance is not standard; smoking/alcohol cessation and working up the stricture are the priorities
All chronic-pancreatitis patients require lifelong 6-monthly EUS surveillance
CA19-9 is a reliable screening test, especially with biliary obstruction
Chronic pancreatitis carries no increased cancer risk
items.explanation: Routine imaging/tumour-marker surveillance in sporadic chronic pancreatitis is not evidence-based (reserved for hereditary/familial high-risk groups). The proven risk-reducers are smoking and alcohol cessation, and the actionable oncological question here is benign-vs-malignant of the CBD stricture (MRCP/CT, ERCP brushings, EUS-FNA). CA19-9 is unreliable and falsely raised by biliary obstruction/stent; the risk is increased, not absent.

items.statement: Patients with type 3c diabetes are protected from severe hypoglycaemia because of intact counter-regulation.
items.explanation: False. Type 3c diabetes involves loss of glucagon-secreting alpha cells as well as beta cells, so counter-regulation is impaired and patients are at increased risk of severe, prolonged hypoglycaemia — a key reason to avoid sulfonylureas and titrate insulin cautiously.

items.statement: Successful ABPA treatment is defined by normalising the total IgE into the reference range.
items.explanation: False. Total IgE is followed as a per-patient trend; response is a ~25–35% fall to a new baseline with clinical/radiological improvement. Most treated patients never normalise IgE, and aiming to do so leads to steroid over-treatment.

items.assertion: Insulin requirements should be increased pre-emptively when starting oral steroids for this patient's ABPA.
items.reason: Systemic glucocorticoids raise blood glucose, predominantly post-prandially.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because systemic steroids cause largely post-prandial hyperglycaemia, a patient already at HbA1c 10% will need higher (mainly bolus) insulin doses anticipated as the steroid starts, then tapered down with the steroid. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: A 6-monthly CA19-9 is a reliable way to screen this patient for pancreatic cancer.
items.reason: CA19-9 is specific to pancreatic malignancy and is unaffected by biliary obstruction.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. CA19-9 is neither sensitive nor specific enough for screening, and it is non-specifically elevated by biliary obstruction/cholestasis — which this patient has from his CBD stricture and stent — making it especially unreliable here. Hence option 4.

items.question: Outline a safe starting basal-bolus plan and titration approach for this type 3c patient.
items.answer: Estimate total daily insulin conservatively (~0.4–0.5 U/kg/day given hypoglycaemia-proneness), split roughly 50% basal analogue and 50% rapid-acting analogue divided across meals; his glargine 14 U is a low basal and will likely need increasing. Stop the sulfonylurea and reconsider/stop the SGLT2 inhibitor. Titrate basal to the fasting glucose and each meal bolus to the next pre-meal/2-hour post-prandial value in small steps. Use individualised, not-too-tight targets (fasting ~100–130, post-prandial <~180 mg/dL), relaxed if hypoglycaemia occurs. Add structured self-monitoring or CGM and hypoglycaemia education, and coordinate insulin up-titration with any steroid course.

items.question: Define ABPA response, exacerbation and remission, and explain why 'cure' is avoided.
items.answer: Response: clinical improvement with a ≥25% fall in total IgE at 6–8 weeks and clearing of any infiltrates. Exacerbation: clinical and/or radiological deterioration with a ≥50% rise in total IgE from the patient's nadir. Remission: sustained clinical and radiological stability with no exacerbation for at least 6 months after stopping oral steroids. 'Cure' is avoided because ABPA is a chronic, relapsing immunological disease — the realistic goal is durable remission on the least steroid possible, escalating to azole or biologic therapy if the patient is steroid-dependent or relapses.

items.question: What is the evidence-based approach to cancer risk in this man with chronic pancreatitis and a CBD stricture?
items.answer: Routine imaging/tumour-marker surveillance is not indicated in sporadic chronic pancreatitis (it is reserved for hereditary/familial high-risk groups). The proven, highest-value interventions are complete smoking and alcohol cessation; chemoprevention (aspirin/statin/metformin) is not established. The actionable oncological focus is the CBD stricture — distinguishing benign chronic-pancreatitis stricture from cholangiocarcinoma or pancreatic-head cancer with MRCP/CT, and where suspicion exists ERCP with brush cytology/biopsy and EUS-FNA. CA19-9 is unreliable for screening and falsely raised by biliary obstruction/stent. New-onset or abruptly worsening diabetes can itself herald pancreatic cancer and warrants imaging rather than simply more insulin.

items.stem: Why are sulfonylureas and SGLT2 inhibitors both problematic in type 3c diabetes?
options: A) Sulfonylureas cause hypoglycaemia due to beta-cell depletion, while SGLT2 inhibitors increase euglycaemic DKA risk from insulin deficiency
B) Both drugs are contraindicated in type 1 diabetes, not type 3c
C) Sulfonylureas cause weight gain, while SGLT2 inhibitors increase hypoglycaemia risk
D) Both drugs are ineffective in type 2 diabetes, not type 3c
items.explanation: Type 3c diabetes involves beta-cell loss and glucagon deficiency. Sulfonylureas (which depend on functional beta cells) cause severe hypoglycaemia due to impaired glucagon counter-regulation. SGLT2 inhibitors risk euglycaemic DKA in insulin-deficient, often lean patients with reduced caloric intake or intercurrent illness.
items.distillation_trace:

items.stem: What defines remission in a patient with allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) undergoing long-term monitoring?
options: A) Sustained clinical and radiological stability with no exacerbation for at least 6 months after discontinuing oral steroids
B) Normalization of total serum IgE levels to within the reference range
C) Absence of symptoms for 3 months while on maintenance azole therapy
D) A 50% reduction in total serum IgE from baseline with stable spirometry
items.explanation: Remission in ABPA is defined by sustained clinical and radiological stability without exacerbation for at least 6 months after stopping oral steroids, as per the monitoring guidelines. Normalization of IgE is not a target, and 3 months is insufficient for remission criteria.

items.stem: For a patient with type 3c diabetes, which of the following is the most appropriate initial basal-bolus insulin strategy and titration approach?
options: A) Initiate total daily insulin at 1.0–1.5 U/kg/day, using 70% basal and 30% rapid-acting analogue, continue sulfonylurea, and target fasting glucose <70 mg/dL

B) Estimate total daily insulin at 0.4–0.5 U/kg/day (50% basal analogue, 50% rapid-acting analogue), discontinue sulfonylurea, titrate basal to fasting glucose, and use postprandial targets of <180 mg/dL
C) Start glargine 10 U daily, add lispro 2 U pre-meals, continue SGLT2 inhibitor, and target fasting glucose <80 mg/dL with no CGM use
D) Use 0.3 U/kg/day total insulin (60% basal, 40% bolus), continue metformin, and titrate based on HbA1c alone
items.explanation: Type 3c diabetes requires cautious insulin initiation due to hypoglycemia risk. The correct approach uses conservative total daily insulin (0.4–0.5 U/kg), balanced basal-bolus split, discontinuation of sulfonylurea, and pragmatic glucose targets. Options A, C, and D either use incorrect dosing, fail to address medication adjustments, or employ inappropriate monitoring strategies.

items.stem: What is the evidence-based approach to cancer risk in a man with chronic pancreatitis and a common bile duct (CBD) stricture?
options: A) Routine imaging and tumor-marker surveillance are indicated for all patients with chronic pancreatitis.
B) Chemoprevention with aspirin, statins, or metformin is established as first-line intervention for cancer risk reduction.
C) Evaluation of the CBD stricture via MRCP/CT, with ERCP brush cytology or EUS-FNA if suspicion of malignancy exists, is the highest priority.
D) CA19-9 levels are reliable for screening and should guide further investigation in patients with biliary obstruction.
items.explanation: In sporadic chronic pancreatitis, routine imaging/tumor-marker surveillance is not indicated (reserved for hereditary/familial high-risk groups). The highest-value interventions are smoking/alcohol cessation. The CBD stricture requires evaluation with MRCP/CT, with ERCP or EUS-FNA if malignancy is suspected. CA19-9 is unreliable for screening due to elevation from biliary obstruction.
